excerpt: He is not of our Shīʿa who usurps the rights of others, who oppresses the people, or who lays hold of that to which he has no claim.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya — chapters on the qualities and obligations of the true Shīʿa

excerpt: Maulana Ali was thereupon moved to speak (the Sermon of Hammam — Khutbat al-Muttaqin, on the attributes of the God-fearing).
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler); discourse of Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib
source_location_hint: Sermon 193 (Khutbat al-Muttaqin / Sermon of Hammam)

excerpt: Description of the believer — sober in face yet sorrowful in heart, generous, forbearing, ever-smiling, keen of observation, content, and trusting only in God.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler); discourse of Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib
source_location_hint: Parallel descriptions of the believer (e.g., Sermon 184/Hikam) in Nahj al-Balagha

excerpt: The Ismaili tradition consistently treats ʿabdiyya (servitude) as ontologically prior to risāla, a priority reflected in the shahāda's ordering ʿabduhu wa-rasūluhu — the servant before the messenger.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, discussions of the formula of testimony

excerpt: The prophets and friends of God endure abasement among men for the sake of God's pleasure; their station with Him is thereby established.
source_location_hint: Discourses on the trials of prophethood and the rank conferred by patience

excerpt: True religious assent must arise from free choice; coercion vitiates the worth of the act of submission, since sincerity is the very condition of love.
source_authority: Early Fatimid daʿi and philosopher (d. after 361/971)
source_location_hint: Sections on the voluntary nature of faith and the conditions of valid worship

excerpt: Cosmological harmony rests on a universal principle of correspondence and mutual attraction: lower beings ascend toward their perfection only by submitting themselves to the higher.
source_location_hint: Discussions of cosmological correspondence between the natural and intelligible orders

excerpt: The Kitab al-Walaya gathers extensive prophetic and Imami traditions on the manifold worldly and otherworldly benefits attending love for the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, traditions on the virtues of loving the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq is reported to have taught that wilāya is attained only through scrupulous piety, exertion, truthfulness, the discharge of trusts, kindness to neighbours, and the keeping of covenants.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on the conditions of attaining wilaya

excerpt: Love of the friends of the Imams (muwalat) and dissociation from their enemies (baraʾa) are inseparable obligations; the believer's heart cannot harbour both at once.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, sections on muwalat and baraʾa

excerpt: The Imams are God's proofs (hujaj) over creation and the channels by which His mercy reaches the earth; through them the world is sustained and preserved.
source_location_hint: Majalis on the cosmic function of the Imams as intermediaries of divine providence

excerpt: Outward acts of devotion are not accepted from those whose hearts cling to what God dislikes; the inward turning of heart and mind toward what He loves is the condition of acceptable worship.
source_location_hint: Discourses on the inward conditions of valid worship and the rejection of merely formal devotion

excerpt: A group worshipped God out of desire — that is the worship of merchants; a group worshipped Him out of fear — that is the worship of slaves; and a group worshipped Him out of gratitude — that is the worship of the free.
source_location_hint: Ḥikma 237 (Ṣubḥī al-Ṣāliḥ numbering); the threefold classification of worship

excerpt: I would follow him as the young camel follows the footsteps of its mother.
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 192 (al-Qāṣiʿa); ʿAlī's account of his attachment to the Prophet in childhood

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān opens the Daʿāʾim al-Islām with Kitāb al-Walāya, treating allegiance to the Imam as the first and foundational pillar of Islam, prior to ṭahāra, ṣalāt, zakāt, ṣawm, ḥajj, and jihād — exactly the seven-pillar ordering invoked in this section.
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya (opening)

excerpt: Sincerity of intention (ikhlāṣ al-niyya) is the foundation of every act of worship; an act offered for the sake of any other than Allāh is rendered void at its root, however outwardly correct it may appear.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣalāt, bāb al-niyya wa-l-ikhlāṣ

excerpt: Were the fear of a true believer and his hope to be weighed in the scales, they would be found exactly equal — for the believer stands always suspended between them.
source_location_hint: Ḥikam (Sayings), short maxims on īmān

excerpt: Beware of this world, for it is sweet and verdant, a deceiver that turns away from its lovers. It is like the serpent: smooth to the touch, but in its bite is a deadly poison.
source_location_hint: Khuṭab and Ḥikam on dunyā

excerpt: For the broader corpus of admonitory traditions from Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq on sincerity, contentment, and the conduct of believers toward one another, see the chapters of Kitāb al-Īmān.
source_location_hint: vol. 1, Kitāb al-Īmān (chapters on akhlāq and relations between believers)

excerpt: Silence as the believer's safeguard and the adornment of the wise — a theme repeatedly attributed to Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Bāb al-Ḥikam — sayings on al-ṣamt (silence)

excerpt: Walāya is placed at the head of the seven pillars of Islam and declared the ground upon which the validity of all other acts of worship depends.
source_location_hint: vol. 1, opening of Kitāb al-Walāya

excerpt: Walāya as the inward locus of religion — an act of the heart no outward coercion can dislodge — integral to the Fatimid daʿwa's understanding of its uncoerced and continuous obligation.
source_location_hint: opening sections on the interior meaning of the pillars
